edges.description: Limbic inflammation disrupts memory and seizure-prone temporal-lobe networks.
Cell-surface antibody subtypes can alter synaptic receptor localization, receptor signaling, or neuronal excitability.
LGI1-antibody-positive patients predominantly manifest limbic encephalitis.
Reduced synaptic AMPAR localization impairs hippocampal excitatory signaling involved in memory and seizures.
Disrupted GABAB receptor function can produce seizure-prone temporal-lobe networks.
edges.source: GABAB Receptor Autoimmunity and Neuronal Hyperexcitability

nodes.description: AMPAR autoantibodies bind GluR1/GluR2-containing receptors on hippocampal neurons and reduce synaptic AMPAR cluster number and localization, producing potentially reversible limbic dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: AMPA receptor antibodies in limbic encephalitis alter synaptic receptor location.
evidence.snippet: Application of antibodies to cultures of neurons significantly decreased the number of GluR2-containing AMPAR clusters at synapses with a smaller decrease in overall AMPAR cluster density; these effects were reversed after antibody removal.

nodes.description: First-line treatment commonly uses high-dose corticosteroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, plasma exchange, or combinations, with escalation based on severity, antibody subtype, and tumor context.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: First-line immunotherapies include high dose steroids, intravenous immunoglobulins and plasma exchange.

evidence.reference_title: Immune therapy in autoimmune encephalitis: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Most clinicians use first-line therapy (steroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, plasma exchange), and if severe or refractory, second-line therapy (rituximab, cyclophosphamide).

evidence.reference_title: Brazilian consensus recommendations on the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune encephalitis in the adult and pediatric populations
evidence.snippet: Treatment should be started within the first 4 weeks of symptoms. The first-line option is methylprednisolone plus intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasmapheresis, the second-line includes rituximab and/or cyclophosphamide, while third-line treatment options are bortezomib and tocilizumab.

nodes.description: GABAB receptor antibodies target inhibitory GABAB1 receptors and are linked to prominent seizures, temporal-lobe EEG abnormalities, and memory dysfunction.
nodes.id: GABAB Receptor Autoimmunity and Neuronal Hyperexcitability

evidence.reference_title: Antibodies to the GABA(B) receptor in limbic encephalitis with seizures: case series and characterisation of the antigen.
evidence.snippet: Confocal microscopy showed colocalisation of the antibody with GABA(B) receptors.

nodes.description: High-dose systemic corticosteroids are a core first-line immunosuppressive treatment for autoimmune limbic encephalitis.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: First-line immunotherapies include high dose steroids, intravenous immunoglobulins and plasma exchange.

nodes.description: Intravenous immunoglobulin is used as first-line immune modulation alone or in combination with corticosteroids or plasma exchange.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: First-line immunotherapies include high dose steroids, intravenous immunoglobulins and plasma exchange.

nodes.description: Many patients formerly classified by high voltage-gated potassium channel complex antibodies have antibodies to LGI1 or CASPR2, which correlate with distinct limbic encephalitis, Morvan/neuromyotonia, tumor, and prognosis phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Antibodies to Kv1 potassium channel-complex proteins leucine-rich, glioma inactivated 1 protein and contactin-associated protein-2 in limbic encephalitis, Morvan's syndrome and acquired neuromyotonia.
evidence.snippet: The identification of leucine-rich, glioma inactivated 1 protein and contactin-associated protein-2 as the major targets of potassium channel antibodies, and their associations with different clinical features, begins to explain the diversity of these syndromes

nodes.description: Inflammation predominates in medial temporal and limbic gray matter, explaining the memory, seizure, psychiatric, and behavioral syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: AMPA receptor antibodies in limbic encephalitis alter synaptic receptor location.
evidence.snippet: Limbic encephalitis (LE) is an inflammatory disorder that predominantly affects the grey matter of the medial temporal lobes, amygdala, and orbitofrontal cortex.

nodes.description: Limbic-region inflammation and synaptic antibody effects converge on temporal-lobe networks, producing memory impairment, seizures, confusion, psychiatric symptoms, EEG abnormalities, and MRI signal changes.

evidence.reference_title: Antibodies to the GABA(B) receptor in limbic encephalitis with seizures: case series and characterisation of the antigen.
evidence.snippet: All patients had seizures, confusion, and memory deficits.

nodes.description: Selected relapsing or deteriorating cases may require maintenance immunosuppression after acute treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: For some patients, maintenance treatment with immunosuppressants might be required to prevent deterioration or relapses.

nodes.description: Serum or CSF antibodies against neuronal intracellular or cell-surface antigens identify major mechanistic and prognostic subgroups.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: Serum and cerebrospinal antibodies against neuronal antigens can be detected in most patients.

nodes.description: Plasma exchange is used to remove pathogenic circulating antibodies in antibody-mediated limbic encephalitis.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: First-line immunotherapies include high dose steroids, intravenous immunoglobulins and plasma exchange.

nodes.description: Severe, refractory, or relapsing autoimmune limbic encephalitis can be escalated to second-line B-cell-directed or cytotoxic immunotherapy.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: Second-line drugs include Rituximab and Cyclophosphamide.

evidence.reference_title: Immune therapy in autoimmune encephalitis: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: when patients fail first-line therapy, second-line therapy improves outcomes and reduces relapses.

evidence.reference_title: Brazilian consensus recommendations on the diagnosis and treatment of autoimmune encephalitis in the adult and pediatric populations
evidence.snippet: The first-line option is methylprednisolone plus intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or plasmapheresis, the second-line includes rituximab and/or cyclophosphamide, while third-line treatment options are bortezomib and tocilizumab.

nodes.description: Tumor-directed treatment is required when limbic encephalitis is paraneoplastic or a tumor is identified alongside a neuronal antibody syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Limbic encephalitis and related cortical syndromes.
evidence.snippet: are likely to improve with immunomodulatory treatment (and tumor removal for paraneoplastic cases) and have, overall, a better prognosis.

evidence.reference_title: Immune therapy in autoimmune encephalitis: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: When present, tumours should be removed.
